metadata.title: What She’ll Hit
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Rithanya Chandran
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Rithanya Chandran — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A clean headline like a company missing revenue guidance hides that the real surprise isn't the forecast being wrong. The Mycroft approach grows by starting with what can actually be measured rather than modeling everything at once. Rithanya's job is not to build the complete system but to deliver a measurable, useful piece of it. The Beneish M-Score combines eight financial ratios to estimate the probability that a company inflated its reported health.

artifactLines: A clean headline like a company missing revenue guidance hides that the real surprise isn't the forecast being wrong.
The Mycroft approach grows by starting with what can actually be measured rather than modeling everything at once.
Rithanya's job is not to build the complete system but to deliver a measurable, useful piece of it.
The Beneish M-Score combines eight financial ratios to estimate the probability that a company inflated its reported health.

beats.narration_text: The move from a clean headline like 'Company X misses revenue guidance' to the deeper point that the surprise isn't the forecast being wrong, plus the Beneish M-Score's eight ratios estimating manipulation probability, is what I want to operationalize. The 'start with what can be measured' discipline appeals to me. Can you (1) explain why a missed forecast is less surprising than it looks given base rates, (2) walk through how the M-Score's eight ratios combine into a manipulation probability, and (3) pick the single ratio I should watch first and why?

props.command: The move from a clean headline like 'Company X misses revenue guidance' to the deeper point that the surprise isn't the forecast being wrong, plus the Beneish M-Score's eight ratios estimating manipulation probability, is what I want to operationalize. The 'start with what can be measured' discipline appeals to me. Can you (1) explain why a missed forecast is less surprising than it looks given base rates, (2) walk through how the M-Score's eight ratios combine into a manipulation probability, and (3) pick the single ratio I should watch first and why?
output: You know the story by the time it reaches the headlines
The surprise isn’t that the forecast was wrong
Not the earnings manipulation that eventually reaches the SEC

beats.narration_text: “Company X misses revenue guidance.” Clean. The surprise isn’t that the forecast was wrong.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'What She’ll Hit' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then name the… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.
